data.title: FS KKR Capital: Buy The Tariff Tantrum, Secure A 13.7% Yield
data.description:
Improving credit quality (a lower non-accrual ratio) was the main reason underpinning my bullish investment thesis on business development company FS KKR Capital Corp. (NYSE:FSK) at the start of the year.

The BDC’s performance, however, has taken a backseat to the tariff tantrum that has made a lot of business development companies complete bargains.

With the stock of FS KKR Capital now selling again for a rather substantial (more than 20%) discount to net asset value, I think FS KKR Capital is a value stock waiting to get scooped up.

Shrinking Portfolio, Credit Profile And Deteriorating NII-Based Coverage

On the surface, FS KKR Capital doesn’t look to be a prime target for passive income investors: The business development company has a higher-than-normal non-accrual ratio (which also again rose in the December-quarter) and its net investment income fell in 4Q24 thanks to higher loan repayments.

As a consequence, the BDC’s portfolio has shrunk, which triggered a decrease in its net investment income which in turn precipitated an increase in the BDC’s dividend pay-out ratio.

Despite these facts, however, I think that FS KKR Capital is a promising BDC buy for passive income investors, mainly because the company has upside tied to an improving non-accrual ratio.

Most BDCs that I cover have had non-accrual ratios somewhere between 1.0% and 2.0%, so with a 4Q24 non-accrual ratio of 2.2% (based on fair value), FS KKR Capital clearly stands out from the crowd in a negative way.

Compared to FSK’s history, however, the present non-accrual ratio has much improved and it is less than half of the 4Q23 non-accrual ratio. What makes a high non-accrual ratio appealing, ironically, is that BDCs like FSK have the opportunity to improve their credit profiles by renegotiating loan terms or extending maturities.

Another potential point of contention is the BDC’s net investment income trend, which is pointing south. FS KKR Capital’s borrowers repaid an average of $1.5 billion in loans per quarter in 2024 which led to the BDC having negative net investment activity in 3 out of the last 4 quarters.

Since the end of 2023, FS KKR Capital’s portfolio has shrunk a total of 8%, or $1.2 billion, which has triggered a commensurate decrease in the BDC’s net investment income.

FS KKR Capital’s net investment income in 4Q24 amounted to $171 million, down 14.5% YoY thanks to a decrease primarily in interest income which is the consequence of a falling principal balance. FS KKR Capital’s total interest income in the fourth quarter was $257 million, down 22.5% year-over-year.

The decrease in interest income was partially compensated for by higher payment-in-kind interest income as well as other income. The fall in net investment income is the main reason why FS KKR Capital’s dividend pay-out metrics deteriorated in 4Q24.

A shrinking investment portfolio that produces a lower amount of recurring net investment income obviously poses pay-out challenges and FS KKR Capital experienced such a challenge in the fourth quarter: The BDC’s dividend pay-out ratio for the first time in a year rose to more than 100%.

While the LTM dividend pay-out ratio remained below 100% in 2024, the BDC could potentially run into a dividend coverage challenge in the short-term.

On the flip side, since FS KKR Capital pays a base $0.64 per share per quarter dividend that is supplemented by a variable $0.06 per share per quarter dividend, the BDC could scrap or suspend its supplement in order to relieve pressure on its net investment income.

Because of the drop in net investment income that occurred in the fourth quarter thanks to higher loan repayments and high non-accruals, FS KKR Capital has fallen to the second-lowest rank in the BDC peer group in terms of LTM dividend pay-out ratio.

The LTM dividend pay-out ratio, in my view, is the single most important BDC comparison metric. In the last twelve months, FS KKR Capital paid out 98% of its net investment income, and only Golub Capital BDC Inc. (GBDC) had weaker dividend coverage. The peer group average LTM dividend pay-out ratio is 88%.

Market Plunge Opens Up Buying Window At 0.79x NAV

FS KKR Capital is now selling, once again, for a very much exaggerated discount to net asset value which even under the consideration of the higher-than-average non-accrual ratio doesn’t make much sense.

FS KKR Capital is selling for a 21% discount to net asset value (which stood at $23.64 as of December 31, 2024) which makes the business development company the BDC with the fourth-lowest Price-to-NAV ratio, after Prospect Capital Corp. (PSEC), New Mountain Finance Corp. (NMFC) and Goldman Sachs BDC Inc. (GSBD).

The two best BDCs, in my view, are Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), both of which convince with their credit profiles, particularly BXSL, and with their dividend pay-out consistency over a long stretch of time.

FS KKR Capital could be a compelling passive income vehicle if the business development company manages to gain control of its non-accruals and successively improves its credit profile and by doing so also catalyzed a rebound in its net investment income.

In this particular case, I would not be surprised to see a stock re-rating to net asset value which kind of gives the BDC 26% upside to the implied intrinsic value, in addition to the 13.7% dividend yield.

Why I Might Be Wrong About FS KKR Capital

If the business development company were to experience an ongoing uptrend in its non-accrual ratio or its portfolio were to continue to shrink, then FS KKR Capital has substantial dividend risk as the BDC would almost certainly under-earn its dividend with net investment income.

Since the U.S. economy seems to still be in great shape at the moment, despite the tariff tantrum, I think that FS KKR Capital has more upside than downside which leads to a skewed risk/reward relationship in favor of FSK.

My Conclusion

FS KKR Capital is a better BDC investment choice than the valuation suggests, in my view, but there are certainly some risks that passive income investors should be cognizant of:

  1. FS KKR Capital’s net investment income has been on the decline due to higher repayments in 2024;
  2. The BDC has higher-than-average non-accruals; and
  3. FS KKR Capital did not fully cover its dividend with net investment income in 4Q24.

Counterbalancing these risks are an exaggerated discount to net asset value, an overall improving credit profile (compared to 2023), and the fact that the BDC could suspend its supplemental dividend, creating some breathing room for BDC to defend its $0.64 per share per quarter base dividend. With the U.S. economy still growing and interest rates possibly falling further this year, the lending activity for BDC’s should be positive in 2025.

In my view, the fear-driven market correction opens up a buying window even for business development companies that might not immediately pop up on investors’ radars, like FS KKR Capital. That said, though, FS KKR Capital’s stock is promising and with its credit profile potentially improving in 2025, the BDC actually has a catalyst for a stock re-rating.

data.title: Triton International: Looking For Value In The 8%+ Yielding Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

Triton International is the world’s largest lessor of intermodal containers with a total fleet of 4.1 million containers representing 7.1 million TEU (‘twenty foot equivalent units’, the standard size in the container business). The company was acquired by Brookfield Infrastructure (NYSE:BIPC) (NYSE:BIP) in 2023, which resulted in the delisting of its common shares, but all series of preferred shares are still outstanding, and these could be an interesting security class to focus on income and preferred dividends.

Strong cash flows, but I’m keeping an eye on the distributions to Brookfield

Looking at the financial performance of Triton in FY 2024, we see the total leasing revenue as disclosed by the company was approximately $1.53B, in line with the total revenue in 2023. That being said, the company saw a lower trading margin while its gain on the sale of containers was substantially lower than in the few preceding years as the market prices for used containers has decreased, resulting in a smaller difference between book value and fair market value.

That being said, the operating expenses decreased rather substantially, mainly on the back of lower transaction-related expenses, while the company also reduced some of its other expenses.

The net finance expenses increased by just around 8%, resulting in a pre-tax income of $567M, which ultimately resulted in a $518M net income. The total cost of the preferred dividends in 2024 was $52.1M (unchanged), which means the net profit attributable to the owner of Triton was $466.1M.

It goes without saying it is encouraging to see the company needed just around 10% of its net income to cover the preferred dividends as that is a good and relatively low ratio.

One of the main issues I had (and still have) with Triton is the fact it is streaming up quite a bit of cash to Brookfield. After wiring in excess of $400M to Brookfield Infrastructure in FY 2023, it sent in excess of $600M to Brookfield in 2024.

The reported operating cash flow was $1.11B while the net capex was $555M and the cost of the preferred dividends was just over $52M, resulting in a net free cash flow of approximately $500M. So while the majority of the distributions to the parents is covered by the free cash flow and a small amount of ‘overspending’ on distributions that is streamed up to Brookfield is fine, it remains an element I will be keeping an eye on. It also explains why the total amount of equity on the balance sheet decreased by almost $130M despite generating almost $470M in net profit; the discrepancy is caused by Triton wiring more cash to Brookfield as part of its distribution program.

The new preferred share issue will make the remaining preferred shares less safe

At the end of January, Triton raised $150M by issuing a new series of preferred stock. The Series F preferred shares are also cumulative in nature and offer a 7.625% preferred dividend yield. The newly issued preferred shares can be called from 2030 on.

While you’d intuitively think adding equity to the balance sheet is a good move, it will actually reduce the ratio of common equity versus total equity. Using the year-end 2024 numbers, the total amount of equity would increase from $2.81B to $2.96B, but the percentage of preferred equity versus total equity would increase from just under 26% to just under 30%. That’s not alarmingly high, but given the new issue of preferred shares without calling the more expensive preferred capital, it looks like Brookfield will continue to siphon cash from Triton to its own treasury. There’s nothing wrong with that, but it is a good reason for me to keep an eye on the balance sheet to ensure the preferred shares remain well-covered on an asset level basis.

This results in the updated table below. Based on the share prices at the closing bell on Tuesday, April 15th, the preferred shares all yield at least 8%. Note: I don’t use the yield to call, considering the company hasn’t called any of the three callable series yet.

I currently have a small position in the Series A preferred shares, which are trading with TRTN.PR.A as the ticker symbol, during the turmoil on the markets earlier this month. Based on the table above, the Series A continues to have the highest yield (in combination with the highest call risk, as Triton would obviously call the most expensive securities first) and there is no real reason to switch to another series. TRTN.PR.A is currently trading at just over its call value of $25 and offers a preferred dividend of $2.125 per preferred share per year, payable in four equal quarterly tranches.

Investment thesis

While the preferred dividends still enjoy an exceptionally strong dividend coverage ratio based on the earnings, I think the main risk and the main element to keep an eye on is the governance risk. As Triton has been acquired by Brookfield Infrastructure, the owner’s interests are not necessarily aligned with the interests of its preferred equity holders.

For now, I don’t see a major issue (although I’d like to see the distributions on the common equity to be more in line with the company’s earnings profile), and the preferred equity allows capturing a well-covered 8%+ preferred dividend yield.

data.title: Republic Services: Even More Attractive In Light Of Tariff Risks
data.description:
Introduction

The recent turmoil in the stock market has been notable, to say the least. With tariff announcements sending shockwaves through global markets, finding individual stocks to write about has been daunting, admittedly. While there is no doubt that there are many macroeconomic indicators signaling distress and volatility is likely far from over, investors have been scrambling for what to do with their portfolios. Managing risk is prudent during every type of market but especially right now. Amid all the uncertainty, Republic Services (NYSE:RSG) has quietly held its ground, outperforming the broader market despite an objectively expensive valuation. I originally wrote about this company in December, since which the company has printed a 14.16% total return at the time of writing vs. -11.35% for the benchmark S&P 500. My original thesis centered on RSG's recession-resistant cash flows, promising growth trajectory, and defensive trading history, traits that feel more valuable today than even a few months ago. Given the current macroeconomic environment as we approach earnings, I wanted to revisit this stock and put the original thesis to the test so far. Additionally, I would like to evaluate if the business, despite currently being very expensively valued, could continue to provide a reasonable case as a relative safe haven for equity investors.

Brief Company Overview

Republic Services is the second-largest waste management company in the U.S., just behind Waste Management (WM). The two companies fill roughly 50% of the US waste services market, with RSG comprising roughly 20% of that. Its core business is fundamentally about collecting, disposing, and recycling waste. In recent years, the company has been investing in new ways to monetize recyclables, expanding its portfolio of hazardous waste assets, and the more legacy growth strategy of tuck-in acquisitions of local trash collection companies. As we will return to later in the article, the company is located exclusively in North America and almost entirely within the United States, which is all of a sudden compelling in the current environment of heightened geopolitical and trade tension.

Revisiting the Investment Thesis

The original thesis had several components. While waste volumes do risk softness in recessions, the company's revenue is 80% "annuity-like", making their business highly cash-generative in good times and bad. That core stability is why Republic Services tends to show up on lists of defensive stocks. In my original piece, I talked about how this business has the kind of resilience you want when things get messy, and the last few weeks have proven that point again. Since Liberation Day, RSG has outperformed the benchmark by several hundred basis points, extending YTD outperformance to almost 30%.

Many defensive stocks have lower growth profiles and tend to underperform during bull markets as well, but acknowledged in the original thesis was the fact that RSG and peer Waste Management have outperformed in good times too. The compelling blend of value and growth at a reasonable price (GARP) makes this company stand out in a sea of investment alternatives. The main detractor from a strong investment case here is the premium valuation, as the company has benefited from strong multiple expansion over time. While it proves hard to stomach investing at such low free cash flow yields when the macro outlook for stocks is poor, I do still believe that the company has strong potential to outperform the benchmark on a relative basis throughout these uncertain times. While tariff threats have proven to be a huge disruptor to smooth market functioning and there is plenty of downside risk in the broader market, I believe that they add a new positive element to the investment thesis here that must be acknowledged.

Tariffs Highlight Republic's Defensive Appeal Further

The recent wave of tariffs has been a clear catalyst for a flight to safety. Whether or not they hit GDP in a meaningful way is still up for debate, but the market's reaction says a lot about where investors think risk lies. Companies like Republic, which don't rely on cross-border supply chains or cyclical consumer demand, are suddenly back in focus. The company's revenue doesn't have direct exposure to tariffs and almost the entirety of their operations lie within the borders of the United States, besides a few operations in Canada.

Tariffs certainly have the potential to disrupt some aspects of the business, notably when accounting for the costs of their equipment and trucks. Indeed, in 2018, both WM and RSG were impacted by tariffs on Aluminum and Steel. Additionally, recycling revenues were impacted slightly, as China was a large end market for recycled commodities. RSG's investment in Polymer Centers to create Blue Polymers is evidence of a shift in management's strategy to target domestic end markets for recycled commodities.

With all of these things considered, I believe that tariffs create an additional layer to the investment thesis that might drive relative outperformance in the coming years, despite an elevated valuation.

Financial Performance and Valuation

The most recent earnings were strong, as the company reported a pretty large earnings beat of $0.19 and a slight revenue miss of $28.42M. While analysts expect earnings growth to moderate somewhat in 2025, the company did guide to an expectation of $1B deployed on acquisitions, which essentially reads as "business as usual" to me. Notably, this is almost 3x as much as the $358M they deployed on acquisitions in 2024. As I covered in my original article, the company keeps very little cash on hand, only 24 cents a share, which is appropriate given the annuity-like revenue profile. While it is appropriate to say the business is leveraged with how little cash they have compared to long-term debt, shareholder distributions continue to be appropriate and within scope as retained earnings increased another ~$300M, as it has done for all recent quarters in memory.

One thing that I am hoping to gather from upcoming earnings reports are the performance of the Polymer Centers as they come online and if they are hitting Adjusted EBITDA targets. The Indianapolis Polymer Center is complete and expected to contribute to earnings in the second half of the year, while the Las Vega Polymer Center is expected to be completed in the second half of the year as well. As a reminder, Polymer Centers are expected to generate $220M of revenue at 30% EBITDA Margin by 2028. Since this is a core piece of the ESG thesis for RSG, staying on track for these numbers is material to continued multiple expansion for the stock.

One story that investors might want to monitor is volume trends when assessing an entry. As management noted in the 2024 Investor Presentation, Volume Growth is correlated to Housing Starts Growth by 90% and GDP Growth by 70%. As the outlook for housing starts and GDP growth right now is somewhat muted, if not downright bearish, it will put the pricing power of the business more under the microscope. We already saw RSG deliver strong earnings growth and a big beat last quarter despite slightly lower volumes.

Even more so than my last article, the valuation continues to be daunting for deploying new capital. RSG is trading at nearly 36x forward earnings or 37.58 on GAAP TTM, which is practically an all-time record. Given that management expects to generate between $2.32-$2.36B of free cash flow in 2025, the stock is now trading around 33x FCF or roughly 3% FCF yield. I think it goes without saying that this is exceptionally low. I see it as a vote of confidence that the resiliency of the business model and long-term appeal as an environmentally friendly compounder will deliver solid results for years to come. I very much hesitate to say that it is "overvalued" given such attractive properties, but it is no question that it is at a historically expensive valuation. One reason I believe that the numbers on the Polymer Centers are so relevant is because they have already been priced in. If margins on these projects are to be lower than expected, it might ding the bullish momentum somewhat.

Dividend and Shareholder Returns

Per the notes above on valuation, it should come as no surprise that the company is trading at a historically low dividend yield. Perhaps reaffirming to my thesis here is that this was also true when I wrote about it last year, and the stock has since outperformed. While the yield is now below 1% and expected forward growth of 8-10% places the current yield at a potential Chowder Score less than 10%, this is still a dividend growth story. In 2024, they returned $1.18B to shareholders, of which $490M was through buybacks. I expect more of the same this year, and see no reason given guidance why the dividend won't be raised another 8-ish% next raise season.

Technical Analysis

This is where things get interesting. Technically speaking, the stock is again approaching all-time highs despite the volatility in markets we have seen lately. I have highlighted how the stock traded sideways in the 2022 equity market bear and the potential sideways channel we might enter currently were we to be in another prolonged bear market driven by uncertainty on tariff policy. The stock has already recovered from a sharp drawdown last week, which is a very strong sign to me, indicating resilience and strength. While I can't point to the chart and say with good conscience that where it is today is a red-hot entry for a short-term gain, I think we will soon see testing of the all-time high again. If we were to fail to break out from here, the new support level of $225 is not too far off and would present a truly compelling entry. For readers who are new to my work, you may notice that I don't have too much heartburn about entering a long-term position without technical confirmation. That is my assessment of the current price: I believe it will do well over the long run and am not dissuaded by an imperfect chart. The chart confirms strength more so than anything.

Final Thoughts: Still a "Buy" on Relative Outperformance vs. the S&P 500

Republic Services continues to hold up better than most in a market where everything else feels like it's on edge. That hasn't gone unnoticed, and the stock continues to trade at a premium that reflects its role as a safe harbor.

If it pulls back closer to trend support, I'd view that as a strong spot to add. Nonetheless, it stays on the Buy list, especially for anyone looking to dial down portfolio risk without going to cash. While the current valuation is tough to swallow, RSG gives investors the ability to continue investing in growth while taking a defensive posture.

data.title: The Role Different Bond Classes Can Play During Market Uncertainty
data.description:
Stock and bond markets continue to be volatile as investors contend with uncertainty about trade and the economy. TD Asset Management portfolio managers Alexandra Gorewicz, Rachana Bhat and Anthony Imbesi join MoneyTalk to discuss their outlook for the government, corporate and high-yield bond markets and what role fixed income can play in a portfolio.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: The bond market has taken center stage for investors as uncertainty about trade and the economy rise. Joining us now to discuss the outlook for the fixed-income space and the roles these assets can play in a portfolio is our bond market panel, Alexandra Gorewicz, Vice President and Director for Active Fixed Income Portfolio, TD Asset Management, joins us now; Rachana Bhat, Vice President, Director, and Lead for Investment Grade Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management; and Anthony Imbesi, Vice President, Director, and Lead for High Yield Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management.

And thank you all for joining us. I think this is the first bond panel on MoneyTalk Live. So, thank you for joining us on the show. We've seen a lot of uncertainty, a lot of volatility over the past few days. Alex, talk to us about what you've seen and what's driving the bond markets.

Alexandra Gorewicz: Well, Tony, I wish I could give you a really comprehensive answer. I mean, I think you can realize from the frequency with which US trade policy changes are being announced-- day to day, sometimes hour to hour-- and it makes it really difficult to have a really good grasp on what the bond market is saying and what it's pricing across a whole host of, we'll call it, economic indicators.

The most concise way that I could summarize is that the bond market is really hyper-tuned right now to the weak sentiment, on growth in particular, that we see in both business and consumer surveys. So the bond market is worried that there are downside risks to US growth as well as global growth.

But there are a lot of competing narratives that actually make it really hard to say with certainty that the bond market is pricing a recession, or that it's pricing inflation, or that it's pricing stagflation or soft landing. I mean, it's not really pricing any one of these things. So, I'll give you a couple of examples on growth.

There are a lot of people who say that the longer these tariffs stay in place or the longer the uncertainty around tariffs stays in place, the more likely it is that a recession can happen in the US and globally. But the bond market's only pricing three Fed cuts to the end of this year. We came into this year pricing one-- one versus three, not a material difference, certainly not pricing recession.

If we look on inflation, there are some who say that the reason we don't price more Fed cuts for this year is because tariffs are expected to induce inflation. And in fact, if we look at those survey indicators, we do see a lot of inflation expectations rising. Although, I would caution there's a lot of partisan bias in that.

But what is the bond market saying? Well, the bond market, if we look at either bonds or swaps that are linked to inflation pricing, they're saying inflation could rise or will rise this year, possibly next year. But beyond that, when we look at five-year, 10-year, or 30-year inflation expectations, they've actually all collapsed.

So really, what the bond market is saying is maybe near-term inflation pressures, but longer term, certainly disinflation, possibly even more if we get a recession. So again, there's nothing clear in terms of any one of the competing narratives in terms of what the bond market is signaling. In Canada, it's a little bit of a cleaner we'll call it narrative because we have a head start on the rest of the world in terms of dealing with US trade policy uncertainty, and so much so that it's actually resulted in job losses in March.

Bank of Canada has cut two times so far this year. But even there, when we look forward, bond markets only expecting one, if maybe two more, Bank of Canada rate cuts-- again, not screaming recession. Now, we're also in the middle of an election. And whether it's investors or the Bank of Canada, I think everybody's waiting to see what kind of fiscal support or fiscal stimulus will come out post-election. And that has to be obviously factored into economic forecasts. But so far, the bond market, even here at home, is in limbo.

Anthony Okolie: OK, so given that backdrop, Rachana, how about investment-grade? How is that holding up in this sort of weaker growth environment.

Rachana Bhat: Sure. And let's start with what Alex highlighted, which was that we are operating in an economy where the risks are above average, both in terms of inflation concerns and growth concerns, really being driven by the uncertainty around tariffs. So you've seen all risk assets reprice and adjust to this backdrop. Some have moved more than the others.

Now, when you turn to the corporate bond market, one of the key valuation tools we look at is spreads. Spreads are the extra yield or the extra compensation you earn when investing in a corporate bond over a government bond because you're taking on additional risk. And when I say spreads are widening or going up, it's the same thing as saying equities are falling, which means it's a bad thing, and vice versa. So spreads tightening or spreads falling is a good thing.

Now, when you look at the moves in the IG land, investment-grade land, year to date, the spread move has been pretty decent. But it's important to keep a perspective to how these spreads have moved historically. And if you look at history and look at current spreads, we are still tighter than long-term averages even, in both the US and Canada.

So when you align this with the fact that the economy is still seeing above-average risks, the valuation doesn't align well still. So our view is the peak in spreads is still ahead of us. But that peak is likely going to be lower than previous recessions.

So let's call it a wide, a very wide-- a major widening event, where spreads blow out. And spreads sustained at those high levels is a very unlikely event at this time. And there are a few factors that are providing the offset, and it's important to understand those factors.

So the three key factors are the first one is demand. So, yields are very attractive from how they are historically and where they stand today. So, when you look at yields, the income that you're earning on bonds, it's at levels you've not seen in many, many years. And that's drawing strong interest from buyers. All they need is for this volatility to subside.

The second factor is supply hasn't kept up with this demand. Even so, there's more so this year. Because of the uncertainty, companies have been hesitant to come into this-- issue in this environment. M&A has been quiet. And M&A typically brings more supply. So, the supply hasn't kept up with demand.

The third factor, credit fundamentals. I mean, they're very solid going into this uncertainty. So, when you have-- and one of the examples I would provide for credit fundamentals is, if you look at the riskiest part of the IG market, which is BBB- rated credits, those are at their lowest share in the IG market than they have been in the last 25 years.

So you've seen these factors support market momentum in real time. So last week, when we saw a very strong risk-off sentiment, you saw spreads widen a lot. But the move tighter after was just as forceful. And that's a sign of how much cash there is on the sidelines waiting for an opportunity. And that needs attention. We need to pay attention to that.

So, as we sit here today, we do believe that spreads need to reprice fairly still. So they need to widen more. But the setup doesn't point to a disorderly blowout in the investment-grade land. And it is in these moments of dislocation where some of the best, call it long-term opportunities, quietly start to build. And our teams are very focused on finding those opportunities at this time.

Anthony Okolie: OK, Anthony, Rachana pointed out to the yields spreading out. What's the impact on the high-yield market? What's been happening there?

Anthony Imbesi: Yeah, there are a lot of similarities to what Rachana was talking to. But I think your opening line pretty much summed it up. There's a lot of uncertainty and a lot of volatility. And that's what we're seeing in the high-yield market right now.

Investors are finding it very challenging to digest, process the constantly changing information that they're getting on tariffs, which makes it pretty challenging to assess credit valuations. The high-yield market has sold off in light of the announcements of the tariffs, to no surprise.

I think what we're seeing is most of the sell-off is coming from the lower-quality, higher-risk companies. So these are the companies that have the most leverage or the highest levels of debt on their balance sheet, most exposed to tariffs or consumer discretionary spending. And the longer the uncertainty of tariffs continues, the more that will be reflected in economic growth, potential higher risk of recession. And ultimately, that gets reflected in deteriorating earnings, corporate earnings, which are a big driver of high-yield credit performance.

In terms of the valuations that we're seeing in high-yield right now, again, Rachana spoke about spreads. High-yield is the same thing. We talk about spreads when we're talking about valuations in high-yield. And it's just really the difference in yield between high-yield bonds and government bonds. So, it's the premium that investors demand in order to lend money to these companies that have lower ratings than government bonds.

And what we've seen is, spreads have widened quite significantly. At the worst point they went, they were wider than 100 basis points, which is probably the worst widening we've seen going back to COVID back in March 2020. They widened to about 460 basis points. Today, they're sitting at about 425.

And for context, I would say, if you look at the post-Global Financial Crisis, spreads have averaged about 450 basis points. So we're inside of that, which, again, doesn't really indicate things are that bad. The other indicator is-- and high-yield spreads are usually a good indicator of when recessions are going to come. We need to get to about 700 basis points before we really start to get into recession territory.

Anthony Okolie: We're nowhere close to that right now.

Anthony Imbesi: Nowhere close to that, and that's also encouraging. And I guess the final thing I can say is, if we go back to March, just before all this tariff announcements, yields were about 7.5%, on average, for high-yield. Today, they're sitting at 8.5%. So they've widened a bit, looking more compelling, could go higher. Again, we're probably not at the bottom right-- at the peak right now. But is just something to keep in mind when looking at high-yield.

Anthony Okolie: That historical perspective is very comforting with respect to the spreads. So with that environment, what role can the different classes of bonds play right now? And Alex, we'll start with you.

Alexandra Gorewicz: Well, and this is really an extension of what you've heard from both Rachana and Anthony, and that is that we're not signaling recession across the bond market or across bond asset classes. And as a result of that, I would just say investors need to be very cautious about taking longer US duration risk. And when I say longer US duration risk, so I started off by telling you that the bond market is hyper-tuned to that weak sentiment. And you have seen two-year and five-year bond yields come down relative to where they were, say, at the start of this year - when everybody thought soft landing, here we come, right?

10-year and 30-year bond yields, though, might surprise a lot of people. They're plus or minus unchanged from where we were at the start of the year. And we think that's fair when you take into account that, again, inflation fears have not yet resulted in strong CPI prints in the US. Growth fears have not yet translated into negative labor or job losses accumulating.

And so, you're really-- the bond market is really stuck between a rock and a hard place. But then when it comes to US government bonds specifically, and again, looking at longer-dated bonds, you're now compounding it with the additional concerns around fiscal sustainability. We're closely monitoring the budget bill and how it's working its way through Congress. We have to see what kind of spending cuts are ultimately going to be incorporated into the final bill.

But right now, what's-- we'll call it part of the budget resolution process, I see trillions of dollars worth of permanent income tax cuts. I see only a paltry few billion worth of government spending cuts. We need to see a lot more balance there. Otherwise, I think investors will be worried about US debt sustainability, and that will impact longer-dated interest rates in the US bond market.

Anthony Okolie: And Rachana, maybe I'll pass it to you next. Talk about how investment-grade bonds, what role they play as an asset class.

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, sure. So we spoke about an environment where yields are high, historically. Credit spreads can still widen a bit more from where they are today. And we also established that technicals, which is the supply and demand aspect of the investment-grade or high-grade market, is in good shape.

So when you put all of those things together, what you want to do is you want to be earning those yields. So you want to be earning that income. But you don't want to be taking too much risk at this time in terms of credit risk and interest rate risk, as Alex highlighted before. There is a lot of uncertainty out there.

So you want to avoid long-term corporate bonds, and you want to avoid fundamentally weaker credits because the price loss there will be much more when the markets get choppy again. So what you want to do instead is you focus on short-term corporate bonds, which allow you to earn the income but it's not too long in terms of duration or tenor of the bond itself, where your price loss will be substantial.

So short-term corporate bonds have decent cushion in how much income you generate. And that income is able to nicely offset some of the price loss you might get in a situation where markets are choppy again. And we believe that at this time what might make sense is focusing a little more on Canadian corporate bonds. The reason being, our economy has more monetary and fiscal policy room to provide some offset that's probably not as much in the US just yet. So, short-term corporate bonds, five-year and under, focusing on Canadian corporate bonds from a tactical standpoint is how I believe investment grade can fit within diversified portfolios.

Anthony Okolie: And Anthony, what should investors be focused on in the high-yield market?

Anthony Imbesi: Well, the high-yield market is-- I would say the most effective way to use high-yield is to include it as part of your fixed-income solutions. So, to what Alex and Rachana just said, if you add high-yield to a fixed-income portfolio of just traditional government bonds and investment-grade bonds, you'll add diversification. You're adding, obviously, higher yield just to get compensated for the credit risk. You're lowering the correlation of your portfolio.

Naturally, high-yield has a lower duration than investment-grade. The bonds aren't-- they have maximum 10-year maturity, and they're callable. So, you're lowering the ratio of your portfolio. If you include loans, you can lower that further. Loans don't have interest rate exposure, as the coupons reset every three months.

But also, it's a big market, $1.3 trillion in size for the high-yield bond market and for the leveraged loan market. You can diversify across many industries. And yeah, ultimately, if you have a long-term view, you can take advantage of these yields of returns. Because if you think about it, if you go back 25 years, the average annual return on high-yield is just under 7%. And it's hard to get that in government bonds and investment-grade bonds alone, so it's a great complement to that. And I guess I would say, yeah, that pretty much sums it up.

data.title: KEPCO: Tailwind From Lower Energy Prices
data.description:
Last week, I highlighted EZCORP (EZPW) as a potential beneficiary of President Trump's tariff uncertainty. EZCORP's focus on providing pawn loans to low-income consumers may stand to benefit, as tariffs are likely to lead to weakening economic growth and tougher financial conditions for consumers.

In my opinion, rather than blame 'the markets' for one's stock losses, it is much more productive to recognize changes in the macro environment and shift one's focus to look for potential beneficiaries. In this article, I highlight another potential beneficiary of Trump's tariffs, Korea Electric Power Corporation (NYSE:KEP).

KEPCO is the dominant South Korean power utility, with a 65% market share in energy generation and a 100% market share in power transmission and distribution. Given the company's generation assets are ~50% fossil-fuel based and the outlook for depressed energy prices as a result of Trump's tariffs, KEPCO may see continued strong earnings in the coming quarters.

I rate KEPCO a buy.

Company Overview

Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEP) or KEPCO for short, is the largest electric utility in South Korea, 51% owned by the South Korean government (Figure 1).

KEPCO has a monopoly in the transmission and distribution of electricity in South Korea and a dominant 65% market share in the generation of power (Figure 2).

Profits Were Squeezed By Higher Energy Prices

Looking at KEPCO's generation assets, KEPCO is predominantly a coal/nuclear/LNG generator, with the three fuel sources comprising 36%, 48%, and 12% of KEPCO's generation mix in 2024 (Figure 3).

As a result of the reopening of the global economy post the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical tensions (primarily Russia/Ukraine war), energy prices surged in 2021. This caused a major crisis at KEPCO, as the company was heavily reliant on fossil fuels to generate electricity, but due to its public-utility nature, the company was not able to raise power prices to consumers at a fast enough pace to compensate for higher input costs (Figure 4).

Finally Turned A Profit In 2024

However, after several years of gradually increasing power prices and the moderation of energy prices in 2024, KEPCO was finally able to record a consolidated profit in the most recent fiscal year (Figure 5).

The biggest driver of KEPCO's return to profitability has been lower fuel and purchased power prices, which declined by KRW 8.8 trillion (~$6.2 billion dollars), as the unit cost for coal, LNG, and oil plunged in 2024 (Figure 6).

Lower Fuel Prices Set To Benefit Profits

Looking forward, with President Trump's tariffs casting a pall on global growth prospects, energy prices have plunged in the last few weeks. For example, Brent Crude, the global oil benchmark, has dropped ~20% in the last few weeks to the low $60s / bbl range, from 2024's average price of ~$80 /bbl (Figure 7). LNG prices are heavily dependent on Brent Crude prices.

Similarly, Newcastle Coal, which is the benchmark for coal primarily used in power generation, is also seeing prices reach lows not seen since late 2021 (Figure 8).

If these lower fuel prices persist, we should expect KEPCO to report a bumper year of profits for 2025, since consumer power tariffs usually lag prices significantly.

Cheap As Chips

Another reason I am bullish on KEPCO is that market valuations are not giving the company credit for the turnaround in its operations. Currently, KEPCO is trading at only 4.4x trailing P/E, which is a fraction of what utilities trade at in North America (Figure 9).

More importantly, if I am correct that KEPCO will likely see an improvement in profits in 2025 as a result of lower fuel costs, we may see KEPCO's valuation multiple expand as investors become more comfortable with the company's earnings.

Could KEPCO Reinstate A Dividend?

KEPCO is a variable dividend payer. In years past, when profits were strong, KEPCO paid a healthy dividend. For example, in 2021, when KEPCO reported $3.8 billion in operating profits in 2020, the ADRs paid $0.54 / share in dividends (Figure 10).

Given strong operating profits in 2024 and the potential for a bumper year in 2025, I believe the likelihood is high that KEPCO will reinstate its dividend in the coming quarters.

A fellow analyst suggests a 6.5% forward yield for KEPCO shares, although I have to admit I am not well-versed enough in Korean equities to have a view on the ultimate dividend rate.

Risks For KEPCO

In my opinion, the biggest risk for KEPCO is a global recession that causes financial crises across the world. While KEPCO's electric utility business is relatively immune to the gyrations of the markets, we have to understand that KEPCO is majority-controlled by the South Korean government. In a financial crisis, I suspect the South Korean government may force KEPCO to dramatically lower power tariffs in order to 'stimulate' its economy. This could result in lower revenues for KEPCO and commensurately lower profits.

Conclusion

In my opinion, while President Trump's tariffs may be bad for the global economy, there are still pockets of opportunities for those willing to look. For example, I believe KEPCO stands to benefit tremendously, as tariff anxiety will likely lead to lower fuel prices in the upcoming quarters. With ~50% of its generating assets reliant on coal and LNG, KEPCO should see a boost to earnings in the coming quarters.

I rate KEPCO a buy.

data.title: The Fed Can't Stay Silent On The Risks Of Rate Manipulation
data.description:
NEW YORK (April 14) - Last week, bond yields spiked 45 bps on the 30-year bond in less than a week, from April 4th to April 10th. The spike ended after President Trump announced a pause on the "reciprocal tariffs" around noon on April 9th.

https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/114309144289505174

Many have speculated that the president eased his hard line on tariffs in response to the spike in the sell-off in both bonds and equities. Investors going to cash, it was feared, could have caused a liquidity crisis in the repo market.

The spike could have happened for a variety of reasons, including foreign investors stepping back from the USD (as illustrated by the DXY for the same period and as discussed here by SA Contributor James Picerno); the basis trade, explained in detail by SA Editor Jason Capul; and speculation that the purportedly fiscal hawks in the Republican Congress' acquiesced to an additional $5 trillion in debt raised the risk premium.

But several commentators have suggested that the spike was attributable to China selling off its USD Treasury bonds as retaliation for the Trump Administration imposing unilaterally high tariffs on China. By selling off some of its substantial holdings of US Treasuries, China, it is reasoned, would cause prices to bond decline and yields to increase. This, in turn, would cause US interest rates to spike, including the critical 30-year mortgage rate, illustrated by Zillow, here.

Unfortunately, we won't know whether China was retaliating by selling bonds until at least May and, likely, not until June. As Bloomberg's Ruth Carson and Masaki Kondo wrote last week,

...Chinese authorities will release April foreign-exchange reserves data on May 7, investors will have to wait until May 30 to see an actual change in securities held in reserves. A clearer indication can be found from the US government’s portfolio flow data due June 18, meaning as of now, no one can tell for sure if some of China’s Treasuries holdings have been sold. [Emphasis is mine.]

Where was the Federal Reserve when rates were spiking?

That uncertainty of the cause of the spike notwithstanding, one should ask: Where was the Federal Reserve when rates were spiking? Why didn't they act?

The first of the Fed's dual mandates is price stability, along with full employment. But spiking bond yields risks both measures. It inhibits new home purchases and new home construction, to say nothing of the knock-on effects in the wider economy.

When it became evident yields were spiking in reaction to US trade policy, the Fed could have stepped in and offered to buy Treasuries at whatever price was necessary to maintain rate stability in mid-, and long-term, bonds.

The tactic, called Yield Curve Control, would not have necessarily resulted in the kind of M-2 growth (and concomitant inflation) that Quantitative Easing - another Fed purchase of US securities - caused because bondholders would know that their bonds could be held or sold; they would be indifferent to selling. Indeed, if the rate spike was actually caused by Chinese retaliation, having the Fed stand ready to buy China's bonds would likely have deterred China's dumping of them in the first place, as it would have had no effect.

I have long doubted the Fed's often proclaimed "political independence"; I'm old enough to remember Nixon Era Fed Chair Arthur Burns. I also remember the startlingly inappropriate Bloomberg op-ed by former New York Fed President William Dudley, where he urged his former Open Market Committee colleagues to "refuse to play along with an economic disaster in the making" that was, in Dudley's view, President Trump's first term tariffs on China. (I note, too, that Dudley suggested a 50 bps rate cut in the September 2024, Fed meeting, just weeks before what was then a 2024 presidential election.)

But how Dudley or Powell or any other Fed FOMC participant may feel about how this or any other president exercises his authority, it is not their job to interfere with it or, by benign neglect, to assist a foreign adversary in undermining an elected president's policies. The administration of foreign and trade policy is the discretion of the elected president and Congress. Monetary policy is at the discretion of the Fed.

I believe that when it's unclear whether a nation like China - which has not been at all shy about challenging US hegemony - may be using measures to undermine US trade policy, as directed by an elected US president, the Fed has a responsibility to step up and employ countermeasures that inhibit the adversary nation's efforts. It need not be a "break the glass" moment that might shock the capital markets, but, instead, merely expressed as a temporary deterrent to make clear that the markets, not China, will set US mid-, and long-, term interest rates.

Secretary Bessent told Bloomberg in an interview today that he "saw no evidence China was dumping bonds", and even that unspecified "foreign investors" were buying bonds. That may be. But to deter China's efforts to undermine the United States in this current trade dispute, the Fed does no harm by standing ready to effect the purchase of bonds to stabilize rates if China dumps bonds to boost US interest rates.

The late Tom Clancy, author of "The Hunt for Red October" submarine warfare novel, might compare Chair Powell standing ready to employ yield curve control to keep rates stable to a submarine commander giving an order to "open the torpedo doors and flood the tubes", but not firing. It's called deterrence. And the Fed should be doing it.

_________________________________________

Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political / geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming and include strategies that we would recommend were the companies our clients. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions with respect to such companies here, however, assume the company will not change.)

If you like our perspective, consider following us by clicking the "Follow" link above.

data.title: U.S. Retail Sales Bounce As Savvy Consumers Buy Ahead Of Tariffs
data.description:
By James Knightley

Spending surge to give way to a likely slump

US retail sales jumped 1.4% month-on-month in March, exactly in line with consensus. It's clear from high frequency reports that many consumers in the market for 'big ticket' imported items brought forward their purchases to get ahead of feared tariffs. We already knew that the volume of auto sales surged nearly 11% last month and this translated into a 5.3% MoM jump in value of sales in today’s report. Electronics didn’t bounce as much as we had suspected it might though, rising a paltry 0.8% MoM while internet sales rose just 0.1%.

The 'control group' which excludes volatile items such as autos, building supplies (+3.3% MoM – metals subject to tariffs), gasoline (-2.5% on falling fuel prices) and dining out (+1.8% on better weather) was softer at 0.4% MoM growth versus the 0.6% consensus. February's 'control' number was revised a little higher from 1% to 1.3%, but given this metric correlates strongly with broader consumer spending patterns that feed into GDP it is a little disappointing. Remember that these numbers are all month-on-month changes in the nominal dollar value of sales. They need to be deflated by the price index that will give a smaller volume growth figure to be plugged into GDP estimates.

The reports suggests that while we are likely to avoid a negative first quarter GDP print, it will only just scrape above zero and the potential rebound in the second quarter is unlikely to be huge given tariffs are soon going to be noticed at a time when consumer confidence is under pressure on three fronts: 1) anticipation of squeezed spending power after tariffs push up prices, 2) worries about a weakening jobs market amidst government spending cuts and 3) declining wealth after the sell-off in equity and bond markets.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
