data.title: QQQ: Not Generational, But Not Bad Either, Buy
data.description:
Current drawdown vs history

I've been writing this article over the past few days, I've had to update the "percent off high" chart a few times as the moves in the Nasdaq are very volatile right now to the up and downside. We currently stand at -14.78% off the all time highs for the NASDAQ proxy, Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ).

I've written several articles regarding broad-based S&P 500 (SP500) ETFs and mutual fund strategies. Many of them have received a bit of pushback about how you would have been better off in the Nasdaq. It's hard to disagree. Here's a total return comparison for the three major index ETF proxies, the Dow 30, S&P 500, and Nasdaq for the entire existence of QQQ:

Tech index investing is not a new thing. This index fund age-wise is right up there with the rest of the oldest index fund ETFs. Here are the inception dates for comparison

  • QQQ:1999
  • SPDR® Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA): 1998
  • SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY): 1993

So we now have over 25 years of head-to-head data. The outperformance is not an anomaly. I have also been accumulating, especially on healthy downturns like the one we're in now, although I'm still far more overweight in S&P 500 index funds like Vanguard's (VOO).

There have been a lot of generational opportunity themes floating around the internet regarding buying opportunities in tech names or index funds. We forget how brutal the tech smackdown of 2022 was when rates started to ramp up very fast. That was generational, this period is only semi-cheap so far.

Vs recent history

The maximum drawdown of QQQ was 32.58% from January 1, 2022 to December 31, 2022.

So for context, we had a nearly -35% drawdown at one point in QQQ during that fabled year. That was the year you could buy Google (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN) and Meta (META) all for under $100 a share.

Oh yeah, let's also not forget NVIDIA at $14.61 adjusted for splits:

The best thing here is rates are almost assuredly on a downward trajectory as opposed to 2022. The discount factor and borrowing costs for many companies are about to go down but tariffs make margins still a thing of uncertainty.Tech is highly affected here as their premium multiples are based on higher-than-market average growth rates, of course, due to margins.

A lot of what the Nasdaq has to offer in terms of margin is strictly high-value products and software services that took many years of huge R&D spending to establish. But depending on the company, they can be highly affected by tariffs related to electronic components and needs for rare earth minerals, for which China may be moving to shut down exports.

If you're still building products in China or adjacent non-export ban countries, the ban probably does not matter. If you're building rare earth mineral-needing products in the US, it probably does matter unless new supply chains can quickly be established.

MP Materials (MP) who owns rare earth mines in Southern Nevada/Southern California is thought of as a possible savior for US-based needs. They will need to ramp up fast if the US truly de-couples from the China-based supply chain. Combined with lithium mining in Nevada, the state is looking more and more like an important national security focus that the Federal Government needs to pump money into quickly.

The fund most detached from the "Friedman doctrine"

The other item I really like about the Nasdaq 100 is that it is the index most detached from Milton Friedman's idea that a company's only responsibility is to produce more profits for its investors. While the highest top-line growth rates and for some companies, bottom line, are found in the Nasdaq, they do generally defer profit for innovation. This can be seen in the research and development [R&D] operating expense lines for the top Nasdaq 100 companies. They spend a greater percentage on that item as a percentage of revenue than other index exposures you could get.

While the above widens out the P/E ratio and makes the index more volatile to discount rates, spending on innovation also grows revenue at a faster clip than those solely focused on profits. Once a company completely captures its customer base, then checkmate, now you can start to expand margins. If your base is large enough and loyal enough to your product, then you can raise prices so subtly that they don't even notice since your product is spread out across such a large base. If your product is pure IP-based software, then the distribution is almost frictionless as well.

Being that the S&P 500 is now also comprised of this "hyper-scaling" model of almost subsidizing your customer and then monetizing later, I do not believe past P/E ratios can apply to present. We now know that the upper limit of what the government and economy can handle in terms of interest rates on the 10-year is somewhere around 5%. A 5% return, risk-free, is now the most we can expect on our money in the future in my opinion.

Being that a 5% taxable yield on a 10-year Treasury has a tax equivalent yield [TEY] of a security providing a near tax-free return [like low to no dividend-paying tech stocks bought to buy and hold] of 6.41% if you are in a 22% tax bracket for example, then the old P/E 15 model gets thrown out the window. In my opinion, a forward P/E of 20 on any index is the new baseline average going forward, as the risk-free rate will never be able to provide more than a 5% taxable yield to the investor [the equivalent of a 20 P/E].

With the forward P/E of the Nasdaq 100 now sitting around 22 X, this is an earnings yield of 4.54%, still higher than the current 10-year risk-free rate at the moment which is also a 5.76% tax-equivalent yield for a 22% US tax bracket range and even greater for those in higher tax brackets. This earnings yield is totally acceptable in my opinion. The most highly weighted companies in QQQ have better balance sheets and current ratios than the US government. The only thing they can't do is print money.

Why I think tech-focused tariffs are very temporary

Most of this recent market correction or mini-crash, has been due to tariffs. Uncertain margins for all US corporations that source from China to make their end product is now just a fact of life. For instance, did you know starting in the early 2000s, almost all synthetic caffeine production was outsourced to Chinese chemical makers? So this could even have repercussions for Coca-Cola (KO), Pepsi (PEP), and especially Monster (MNST). Glad I stick to tea and decaf! Oops, tea is from China too, darn it.

If a company doesn't source something from China, they probably source at least one component of what they make from somewhere else. There's almost no way to unweave the basket, at least as it relates to commodities, widgets and chemicals.

What tech work in China looks like

Rural Chinese factory workers come from the vast farming areas outside of cities and would be expected to make less than $4,000 USD per year if they stayed put. Traveling to industrial centers for work makes them considerably more and they are willing to endure a lot of hardships along the way.

Not all Chinese are willing to do this type of work, in fact very few are. Thus to replicate this in America, the Trump administration will have their work cut out for them.

Robotics is the only way

I recently chimed in on a popular article over the weekend regarding the pause in smartphone and electronics tariffs:

Here's my opinion.. They want solid commitments and movement from Apple, Taiwan Semi, Broadcom, etc.. etc. They know these tech companies make commitments and then stall under the guise of feasibility, permitting, or something.

I watched the Foxconn deal in Trump 1.0 pretty closely as I speculate a bit in Wisconsin real estate and Racine saw a buying boom based on the commitment they gave Trump the first time around. It amounted to nothing. A lot of unhappy mid-west rental property buyers snapped up homes where the job creation didn't happen and the huge project was basically scrapped.

I think these tariff threats will continue until all the tech companies are moving dirt on new production facilities. Unfortunately the tech companies probably also know the economy's reaction to tariffs both in the bond and equities market after this week or so of turmoil. Trump doesn't have as much leverage as originally thought. In the end, only some steep subsidies, massive Federal tax credits, or something will get this done.

There will need to be some sort of bill that says wherever these qualified entities set up shop is automatically an opportunity zone in addition to subsidies. The flaw in opportunity zones is trying to force companies into certain geographies that might not make sense to them. They need to be able to create their own opportunity zones wherever they want based on a national security asset qualification.

This is the chapter where the Trump admin goes from believing they can cut spending and collect tariffs to get out of a debt hole to trying to grow their way out of it and ramp up subsidies. I'm surprised this escalated so quickly.

China also has opportunity zones with tax breaks etc., for manufacturing, they call them Special Economic Zones or SEZs. Shenzhen is the most famous of the lot. Our opportunity zones might be a similar idea if executed correctly. The goal is to attract foreign investment into these zones and opportunity zones need to be set up the same way as SEZs. We need more Taiwanese, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Mexican, etc. manufacturing experts setting up shop in the US and adding robotics to the mix to drive down costs.

The companies with the assistance of the US Bureau of Labor Statistics need to figure out where feasible skilled labor resides and assist them with visas for skilled engineers of foreign origin to ramp up. They should also afford them the ability to create their own opportunity zones wherever they set up based on their function of importance related to supply chains or manufacturing. Do not pigeon hole them into a geography of the US that might turn out to be the wrong one for whatever reason.

What I want to emphasize is even China has to attract their most cheap, rural human capital to worksites to achieve these production costs. The majority of Chinese labor is also not cheap and not willing to endure these conditions. There is a monumental task ahead and only AI, robotics, and a whole lot of tax breaks and incentives will get us there. The Nasdaq 100 and tech companies in general will be paramount to achieving this end goal. All hands on deck.

Risks and summary

As I stated, I think the Nasdaq should have a premium over other indexes as the companies within have higher growth rates and better overall R&D spending as a percentage of revenue. A 22 X forward multiple is not cheap, but also not expensive when you realize that the 10-year risk-free rate likely can't be allowed to exceed 5% for any reasonable amount of time and a 22 P/E is a tax equivalent yield of around 5.7% at a 22% tax bracket in a taxable account.

Margins are uncertain for all companies right now, so buyer beware. I'm starting to think long-term tariffs are a bluff, but who knows? This is not a generational buy, but the haircut was nice and makes QQQ worthy of adding in correction territory.

data.title: Why Hugo Boss Might Be A 'Buy' For The Long Term Here
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Since I follow other luxury conglomerates such as LVMH (OTCPK:LVMUY), it shouldn't be a surprise that I also keep a close eye on what I consider to be luxury-peripheral brands.

Hugo Boss AG (OTCPK:BOSSY) is one of those, and one with a somewhat humorous ADR ticker. While I have not been interested in buying the company, with the exception of one time a few months ago (and I didn't pull the trigger), the recent bout of decline in the company's share price coupled with not much worse estimates in terms of its earnings caught my interest.

I'm always on the lookout for cheap valuation potentials with good upside. However, the last few years and a few failures in the space to estimate "proper value" have left me a bit burned by the potential of consumer discretionary and cyclical sectors, such as clothing. It's only through wrappers of luxury companies that I have invested in them - at least until I recently bought Hennes & Mauritz at a low, more typically known as H&M.

Well, in this article, I will be looking at establishing a conservative valuation for Hugo Boss and see if the company can be argued to be an attractive purchase at this price - and if so, do I buy it here?

Let's take a look.

Hugo Boss from A to Z

Hugo Boss has a strong position in the clothing market, both locally and internationally. In particular, the company has the obvious appeal for premium menswear. I will be clear though in that saying that I do not personally wear Hugo Boss - it's not my style - but I know many people, including family members, for whom this brand is one of their "go-to" brands.

When it comes to clothing companies, I believe you want a few things. You want as much control of manufacturing and distribution as possible. If you're buying somewhat that outsources most of their production, their margins are not trustworthy for the long term given that they are at the mercy of their production and can't deliver efficiency in the same way. You also want a clear angle, which Hugo Boss has in the premium menswear category.

That is attractive.

I wouldn't go so far as to call Hugo Boss "luxury." To me, luxury is something like a Brioni or a Brunello Cucinelli (OTCPK:BCUCY) - but it's certainly a premium brand deserving of attention for that category. Having said that, the premium/luxury wear market has a high degree of fragmentation, with a lot of short-term changes in fashion. This requires a player to maintain flexibility.

That being said, I believe menswear fashion is far more stable than womenswear fashion. That's one of the reasons I like investing in menswear more than womenswear.

As to the other fundamentals, Hugo Boss has a very high share of control of distribution, which is another key factor - it sells nearly 70% of its products in brick-and-mortar, with 1,400 stores, including 450 freestanding, and has its own online locations. This lack of dependence on other parties should allow the company, in my view, to expand its operating margins provided that something doesn't happen (tariffs, anyone?).

The company's several fundamental advantages are several. Hugo Boss has 10% of the market share in designer menswear, according to Morningstar (Source). Furthermore, the company already owns the manufacturing, distribution, and retail that it needs to dominate much of the market where it's relevant, including China, and the company has the potential to grow online as well.

For the 2024 period, presented little more than a month ago, Hugo Boss generated sales of just north of €4.3B, with EBIT of €365 coming to an EBIT-margin of 8.4%. The company is down because of geopolitical tensions, tariffs, sector slowdown in several geographies, FX volatility with the strong dollar, consumer confidence, and inflationary pressure. You name a headwind, it's likely Hugo Boss suffers from it.

However, there are bright spots. The FX issues are likely to reverse given the historical weakening of the dollar by more than 10% towards both the SEK and EUR in less than two months. This will be very advantageous to Hugo Boss.

And frankly, top-line sales were at an absolute record.

The company has a very strong social media presence, with over 11M followers and plenty of engagement - though I'm unsure personally if the social media part is as important for menswear as women (might just be me being old-school though).

The company is in the investment phase and pushes capital to brand development, distribution, and operational efficiencies.

In terms of specific products, the company is expanding denim, which now makes up more of the company's sales, while at the same time driving efficiencies in retail operations and general admin costs.

While growth trajectories are currently solid, I would quickly point out that the company's results are likely to be impacted by tariffs and other factors, which haven't been factored into these estimates, as they came out before those impacts.

The outlook for the coming year is uncertain, with everything from a 2% top-line decline to an equal amount of top-line growth, with EBIT up, but everything from 5-22%, a very wide range. And again, this does not include tariff impacts. Then again, you can see above that NA/Americas is far less important to the company than other brands, so the impact might not be as severe as some fear.

What I want to emphasize here is the importance of inventing in a company that in terms of production, sourcing and distribution owns as much as possible, if you want to get into clothing investment long-term. Any other mode is too exposed to margin pressure or uncertainties from sourcing.

And Hugo Boss isn't doing everything right either, in my view.

There are some Hugo Boss products I can get on board with, like perfume and denim. Then there are some I view as clear mistakes for the company to get into - like footwear. In my view, Hugo Boss has nothing "to do" in footwear, and the products are not that convincing to me. I understand their aim to diversify given the slow growth in core formalwear segments, as wardrobes across the world seem to be taking a step towards casual wear, but this also brings an advantage to the company's already-impressive casualwear segment.

The fact that this casualwear segment has relatively low entry barriers isn't a worry to me for the distribution and production reason I've mentioned - though we might see some volatility because of this.

The third and perhaps largest risk to the company, especially in the U.S. where this is more common, is the exposure to outlet stores, which hurts the brand's exclusivity. I already don't consider it a luxury brand, but if the company dilutes this further, it could hurt brand perception, and that is not something that you want to do because it usually comes at the cost of high-price to low-price sales.

The tariffs have a potential impact because much manufacturing is done in countries like Thailand. America accounts for around 30% of global luxury consumption, and there are several companies in core sectors or subsectors that are seeing impacts. I believe that, generally speaking, pure luxury can offset tariffs with pricing better than just premium brands. Also, luxury consumers are mobile. The fact is that some people visit Doha, Dubai, Kuala Lumpur, Tokyo, Singapore, Paris, or London to purchase luxury goods, and tariffs for such purchases are moot.

But Hugo Boss, to me, isn't pure-play luxury; it's premium - and that means the forecast is somewhat different than, say, for LVMH.

Let's look at valuation.

Valuation for Hugo Boss conveys an upside, even at a significant discount.

Your takeaway from my introductory segment might be that I am negative on Hugo Boss. That would be wrong. While I don't consider it pure-play luxury, I do consider it undervalued in the now-undervalued peer segment of premium/luxury products. This group involves companies such as Kering SA, Ralph Lauren, and Hugo Boss. We'll get into those specific companies later.

For now, realize that Hugo Boss trades at a multiple of around 10.66x P/E compared to a typical of around 20x. That means at about half. Have earnings taken a hit? Yes, about 18% last year. Is it justifiable in terms of valuation decline?

I would say no.

The company has low debt, is BBB rated, and hits targets more often than it doesn't hit them. Furthermore, it has an average upside currently forecasted at 18-19% per year until 2027E.

As you can clearly see, the company has been punished over the past 2 years and has, as I view it, found its "bottom." It's time to buy the company if you're keen on this sector and a native yield of over 4%. This is excellent for this sector.

The lowest average multiple for the company we can find for the last 20 years is 17.2x P/E. Based on the current 10.66x and an upside to this in 2027E, we find an annualized rate of return of almost 50% and an ROR of 185% in total until then, with an estimated share price of almost €93/share native.

Is this likely?

I would take it with a spoon of salt. It does not, in my view, include tariff impacts and whatever else might come. But it's a suitable "bullish" case. My base case is a 13-15x P/E, representing the inclusion of tariffs, but in fact even at a low 13.5x P/E as average, the company's RoR is still triple digits here.

A suitable bearish case would be to essentially forecast the company lower than it is today, at 8.6x P/E, which implies a massive discount to any long-term upside and ignores the historical average. But even at 8.6x P/E, the company fulfills my return criteria with a 16.11% annualized RoR, or almost 50% until 2027E.

This expresses quite well that I view the company as being undervalued in any scenario, and also expresses why it's a "Buy." It's not the only company in the sector that is a buy this way, though. Peers like Kering (OTCPK:PPRUY) and Ralph Lauren (RL) are also in positions of being pressured - though none of them as cheap or with the same upside as we have for Hugo Boss. My choice until now has been Kering, which I recently added to, but I will now also add Hugo Boss from a peer perspective. The company is what I consider to be truly cheap here.

To remain conservative, I move about 11x P/E on a 2027E forward basis with the current forecast, which comes to a fair value of around €60/share for 2027E, which means that at a price target, or PT, of €45 or below, the company can be bought based on that fair value estimate.

Thus, my PT on Hugo Boss is €45/share.

The ADR BOSSY is a 0.2x ORD ADR, meaning that my PT of 45 is $10.21/share. Keep in mind that the current unprecedented weakness of the USD relative to the EUR is inflating this quite a bit. I would look at investing in the native ticker (FWB: BOSS) where possible, including here.

My introductory thesis for the company is as follows.

Thesis

  • I view Hugo Boss as a fundamentally appealing company, albeit in a tricky space rife with volatility, proven by the currently generally undervalued state of its peers and a 10-year RoR of negative 60%+ for Hugo Boss's native ticker.
  • However, the company has stable earnings, a 4%+ dividend, and an upside to a conservative PT of €45, with a potential 11x P/E 2027E fair value of €60/share for the company.
  • Because of a market-beating upside in every scenario I can see here, I view the company as an attractive "Buy" here and give the same rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €45/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2025. The company would be cheap below €30/share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ultragenyx: Commercial Engine Fuels Rare Disease Pipeline
data.description:
Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:RARE) provides a compelling case study in the rare disease space, moving from a development-stage biotech to a commercial entity with a pipeline that is poised for significant expansion. While the risks of biotechnology investing remain, the company's recent performance, clear guidance, and pivotal regulatory move involving its gene therapy candidate UX111 offer an increasingly bullish picture for investors looking beyond headline net losses. I believe that accelerating revenue growth, disciplined expense management, and a de-risked late-stage pipeline create a good entry point for long-term investors.

Financials Show Strong Momentum

Ultragenyx's financial performance supports its ambitious development plans. The fourth quarter of 2024 generated $165 million in total revenue, up 29% from the same period in 2023. This was no fluke: The full year ended December 31, 2024 followed this trend and generated $560 million in total revenue, up 29% from the prior year. This consistent and healthy top line growth demonstrates the successful commercialization of its core products.

Crysvita remains the core, with $115 million in Q4 and $410 million for the full year 2024, up 23% and 25% year over year, respectively. The $135 million in full-year Crysvita sales from Latin America and Turkey - a 78% jump from last year - is noteworthy. All this demonstrates that management can operate effectively in markets beyond the US/EU strongholds and fill in huge unmet needs while taking the drug into new global markets.

Dojolvi is also contributing $31 million in Q4 (34% YoY) and $88 million for the year (up 25% YoY). That makes it a major second pillar of revenue. Evkeeza is smaller at $10 million in Q4 and $32 million for the full year but shows promise, with the company saying demand is building in its territories outside of the United States following recent launches. This diverse revenue stream from several approved products acts as a buffer and funds innovation.

Aggressive investment in a broad pipeline comes with a price. Operational expenses were $287 million in Q4 and $1,096 million for the full year of 2024. Notable is the large non-cash stock-based compensation component of these figures ($40 million for Q4, $158 million for FY 2024), which slightly inflated the cash outlay perspective. This translated into a net loss of $133 million ($1.39 per share) for Q4 2024 compared with $123 million ($1.52 per share) in Q4 2023. But looking at the whole year paints a more positive picture: The net loss narrowed to $569 million ($6.29 per share) from $607 million ($8.25 per share) in the previous year. This improved per-share loss despite heavy R&D investment suggests growing operational leverage and possibly better share count management.

Crucially though, management is signaling a path to greater financial discipline. Its 2025 financial guidance projects are continuing to show 14-20% revenue growth, translating to a range of $640 million to $670 million. Crysvita is expected to contribute $460 million to $480 million, while Dojolvi contributes $90 million to $100 million. Perhaps most importantly for investors wary of capital, Ultragenyx said it would prioritize expense management to fund investments in future launches and Phase 3 programs. This should result in lower net cash used in operations in 2025 than in 2024 ($414 million). This planned reduction in cash burn comes in addition to the company having $745 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable debt securities as of December 31, 2024, and positions the company well financially to fund several late-stage development programs.

Pipeline

Although the commercial portfolio is the current financial engine, Ultragenyx's long-term value proposition is based on its broad and growing pipeline. Its portfolio includes biologics, small molecules and advanced gene therapies for debilitating rare diseases with high unmet needs. The clinical candidates, apart from the commercially available Crysvita, Mepsevii for mucopolysaccharidosis type VII, Evkeeza for homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, and Dojolvi for long-chain fatty acid oxidation disorders, are the next waves of potential value creation.

Within biologics, UX143 is an asset in Phase 3 trials for osteogenesis imperfecta - a rare brittle bone disease with limited treatment options. A huge market would result from success here.

Yet perhaps the most transformative action could be along the gene therapy pipeline. Ultragenyx has carefully constructed a portfolio of late-stage gene therapy candidates based on AAV vectors to target the causes of several devastating genetic disorders. Now three distinct programs have entered Phase 3 trials: UX111 for Sanfilippo syndrome type A, DTX401 for glycogen storage disease type Ia, and DTX301 for ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency. Also in Phase 2 is the drug UX701 for Wilson disease. Having multiple late-stage gene therapy shots on goal for each rare disease diversifies the clinical risk profile and opens up additional pathways for major value inflection.

Key Catalyst: UX111 BLA

Ultragenyx reported that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration accepted for review its Biologics License Application for UX111 as treatment for Sanfilippo syndrome type A patients. In accordance with the BLA Priority Review, the FDA set a Prescription Drug User Fee Action date of August 18, 2025.

Importantly, FDA also told the company it is not planning to hold an advisory committee meeting on this application. Not necessarily an approval guarantee, the absence of a planned advisory committee is often taken positively, suggesting that the FDA finds the submitted data package relatively straightforward or compelling.

That acceptance places Ultragenyx potentially one step closer to treating Sanfilippo syndrome type A, which has never been done before. In addition, he noted the company's launch readiness and the wider implications that success with UX111 could be a validation of their approach for accelerated approval pathways in other neuronopathic MPS diseases - an important strategic consideration.

The basis for the BLA submission is the latest data from the pivotal Transpher A and long-term follow-up studies presented at WORLDSymposium in 2025. This data showed that the UX111 treatment resulted in rapid and sustained reduction of heparan sulfate levels in cerebrospinal fluid of patients with Sanfilippo syndrome type A at time of treatment, regardless of age or disease progression. These data showed a statistical improvement in the Bayley-III raw scores across subdomains of cognition, receptive communication, and expressive communication in the modified intent-to-treat group versus natural history data from untreated patients. Such clinical endpoints correlated with substantial, persistent decreases in CSF-HS levels. Elevations in liver enzymes are the most common reported treatment-related adverse events to date and are mostly of mild (Grade 1) or moderate (Grade 2) severity and resolve in all.

Such BLA acceptance, in combination with the Priority Review and absence of a planned advisory committee, de-risks the UX111 program and provides a major catalyst for the August 2025 PDUFA date. Such approval would be transformative for MPS IIIA patients and a strong validation of Ultragenyx's entire gene therapy platform and development capabilities.

Valuation and Market Opportunity

Ultragenyx is still in an investment-heavy growth phase. Due to this, I will mostly talk about the total addressable market given the company's focus on rare and ultra-rare diseases for which premium pricing is common and sustainable.

UX111, Ultragenyx's lead gene therapy for Sanfilippo syndrome type A is under FDA priority review. With a prevalence of 1 in 70,000, affected patients range from roughly 500 to 1,000 in the United States and 3,000–5,000 globally. With a possible treatment price of $2.5 million, even 50% global penetration implies a TAM exceeding $3.75 billion. Given the lack of approved treatments and the urgency of this population, uptake could be rapid and substantial.

UX143, in Phase 3 for osteogenesis imperfecta, targets 25,000-50,000 patients worldwide. In a range similar to Crysvita ($300,000–$400,000 per year) - a 25% market share would support over $2.5 billion in annual revenue. DTX401 for GSDIa affects about 6,000 worldwide and may command a similar gene therapy price point, thereby bolstering the pipeline depth. The three programs put together have a combined TAM of $7-10 billion.

This is on top of a commercial foundation worth close to $500 million annually led by Crysvita and growing with Dojolvi and Evkeeza. As Ultragenyx has multiple late-stage programs in markets where high unmet need permits rapid adoption and sustained pricing power, its current valuation may underestimate its true earnings potential. For long-term investors, a growing base business and pipeline-driven options create compelling upside.

Risks and Competition

I am bullish on Ultragenyx's prospects but there are challenges for potential investors. Clinical development is uncertain. Despite promising data, late-stage trials for setrusumab, DTX401, DTX301 or even UX111 may fail to meet their primary endpoints. Regulatory hurdles can come unexpectedly even after BLA acceptance, and approval is never guaranteed until the final decision is rendered. Complex manufacturing processes, particularly with gene therapies, present an ongoing operational risk that must be executed precisely.

On the commercial front, growth is strong but competition is present and will probably increase. Crysvita has potential rivals, and obtaining favorable pricing and market access for existing and new therapies takes work. Market penetration and potential resistance from payers or other stakeholders must be overcome before projected revenue targets can be met.

Despite a reduced cash burn goal, the company is still experiencing a net loss financially. Its $745 million cash balance gives some runway, especially with reduced burn, but delays or setbacks in the pipeline may require additional financing and/or increased debt. The company also faces some concentration risk, as Crysvita controls a significant portion of current revenue.

The rare disease and gene therapy fields are competitive and draw big investment from established pharmaceutical giants as well as smaller biotechnology firms. Yes, rivals are working on therapies targeting similar indications. But Ultragenyx's established commercial infrastructure, experience in rare disease development and multi-platform pipeline position it competitively.

Future Outlook

Ultragenyx offers an attractive investment thesis based on demonstrated commercial success and a high-potential de-risking pipeline. It is consistently posting double-digit revenue growth thanks to the global success of Crysvita and contributions from Dojolvi and Evkeeza. Important financial points have been addressed by management, pointing to reduced cash burn in 2025 with a healthy cash reserve.

Real excitement will come when that pipeline matures, however. With a Phase 3 biologic for osteogenesis imperfecta and three distinct Phase 3 gene therapies advancing, the potential for transformative value creation is enormous. Recent Food and Drug Administration acceptance and the Priority Review of the UX111 BLA for Sanfilippo syndrome type A with PDUFA dates set for August 2025 and no currently planned advisory committee are turning points and important near-term catalysts.

While inherent risks remain, Ultragenyx's execution record, diversified portfolio and improving financial discipline position it well. In my view, the market does not yet fully appreciate the combined potential of existing commercial assets along with the approaching inflection points along the late-stage pipeline - particularly in gene therapy. For investors tolerant of biotechnology risk and long-term horizons, Ultragenyx is an opportunity to invest in a company leading on several fronts in the fight against rare diseases and which has significant growth potential ahead.
